chunk_summary: Guidelines for advancing enteral feed volumes and fortification in preterm infants, balancing nutritional needs with NEC risk, and monitoring for feed tolerance and growth optimization.
learning_objectives: Understand the initial and progressive enteral feeding volumes for preterm and extremely low birth weight infants.
Identify clinical signs of feeding intolerance and appropriate management strategies.
Learn strategies for optimizing growth in preterm infants, including the use of human milk fortifiers and donor milk.
Recognize the importance of standardizing feeding protocols to minimize risks.
key_facts: Initial feeds for stable preterm infants (≥1200 g) start at 60–80 mL/kg/day, advancing by 15–30 mL/kg/day.
Extremely low birth weight infants may start at 10–30 mL/kg/day, advancing by 10–20 mL/kg/day.
Full enteral feeds (150 mL/kg/day) are typically achieved by the end of the first week.
After the first week, preterm infants may require 180–200 mL/kg/day to meet energy needs.
Human milk fortifiers should be used to optimize protein and mineral intake if growth is suboptimal after two weeks.
Donor milk can be used as a bridge when maternal milk is insufficient, with efforts to increase maternal expression.
reference_quote: Enteral feeding plans for preterm infants should balance nutritional urgency against NEC risk and should be standardized within the unit to avoid erratic advancement.
document_key_topics: Enteral feed volume advancement in preterm infants
Human milk fortification and donor milk use
